scenario: You raised BTN, BB calls. Flop A♦7♣2♥ (dry, disconnected). You have Q♠J♠ (nothing).
question: Should you c-bet this dry ace-high board even with nothing?
options: Yes — dry ace-high board favors your raising range; BB's capped range whiffs, high fold equity
explanation: C-bet. The A-7-2 rainbow board hits YOUR range (you have the aces, BB would 3-bet AA/AK) and misses the BB's capped calling range. High fold equity + range advantage = textbook c-bet bluff, even holding QJ. Board texture + range advantage drive this, not your cards.

scenario: You raised, and got THREE callers. Flop 9♥8♥7♣ (very wet, multiway). You have A♠A♦ (overpair).
question: How does multiway + wet board change your overpair c-bet?
options: Bet for value AND protection — but be ready to fold to heavy action; multiway wet boards crush overpairs often
explanation: Number of opponents + vulnerability are key factors. AA is strong, but 9-8-7 two-tone with THREE opponents is dangerous — straights, two pair, sets, flush draws all live in those ranges. Hardin: bet for value+protection to deny equity, but your overpair's relative strength is much lower multiway. Don't stack off light. This is exactly the 'overvaluing one pair' trap on a wet multiway board.

scenario: You raised, one caller. Flop K♠K♦4♣ (paired, dry). You have A♥Q♥ (ace-high).
question: Paired dry board, you have ace-high. C-bet or check?
options: C-bet — paired dry boards favor the aggressor's range and are great bluff spots; caller rarely has a king, folds most of their range
explanation: C-bet bluff. Paired dry boards (K-K-4) are premium c-bet bluffing spots: the caller rarely holds the trips (few Kx in a flat-call range), your range credibly contains kings and overpairs, and most of their range (small pairs, broadway misses) folds. Board texture + range advantage = bet, despite ace-high.

scenario: You raised BTN, the BB (a calling station, fold-to-cbet 25%) calls. Flop 6♠5♠4♦ (wet, low). You have A♣K♦ (two overcards, no pair, no draw).
question: Against a station on a wet low board with AK-high, c-bet?
options: No — wet board smashes a station's wide calling range, and they don't fold (25% fold-to-cbet); no fold equity, no made hand. Check/give up
explanation: When NOT to c-bet: low fold equity + board favors their range + you have no made hand/draw. A 6-5-4 board hits a station's wide range hard (any two pair, straight, draw), and a 25% fold-to-cbet means they won't fold. Firing AK-high here has no value (worse doesn't call) and no fold equity (they don't fold). Check. Opponent tendency + board texture override 'AK is pretty.'

scenario: YOUR HAND. You 3-bet B♥B (wait: QJs) and reach a flop A-J-7 with middle pair (the J). You CHECK the flop as the aggressor (pot control). Turn is a blank.
question: You checked flop with middle pair on an ace-high board. Was that check correct, and what now?
options: Correct pot-control check (ace hits caller's range, your J is medium-strength); now you can call one bet or take a delayed thin-value/showdown line
explanation: Based on your real QJs hand. Checking middle pair OOP on an ace-high board is a CORRECT pot-control play — the ace smashes the caller's range and your jack is only medium strength. This is a legit delayed-c-bet / showdown-value line, NOT a missed c-bet. The leak in the real hand came later (calling big bets with one pair), not from this check. Checking was right.

scenario: You raise CO, BTN (a thinking TAG) calls — you are OUT of position. Flop A♠T♦6♣. You have K♣Q♦ (no pair, gutshot to the broadway).
question: OOP vs a TAG on A-T-6 with KQ gutshot. C-bet?
options: Always c-bet — you have the ace advantage
Small c-bet is defensible (range favors you, you have a backdoor + gutshot), but be ready to give up vs a raise; OOP without a made hand, one-and-done is fine
explanation: Position is a c-bet factor. The A-T-6 board favors your range (you have the aces), so a small c-bet as a range bet is fine — and KQ has a gutshot + backdoors. But you're OOP with no made hand: plan to give up if raised or if called and you don't improve. Don't turn a one-time range bet into multi-street spew without equity. C-bet, then proceed honestly.

scenario: You raise, get TWO callers. Flop J♥T♥9♠ (extremely wet, multiway). You have A♠A♦ (overpair, no heart).
question: Overpair on a super-wet multiway board (J-T-9 two-tone). C-bet?
options: Bet big for value — it's aces
Check or bet small with give-up plan — this board crushes two callers' ranges (straights, two pair, sets, flush+straight draws); AA is now a vulnerable one-pair hand multiway
explanation: Multiway + extreme wetness downgrades even aces. J-T-9 two-tone connects with two callers' ranges enormously: KQ/Q8/87 straights, sets, two pair, combo draws. Your AA holds ONE pair with no heart — its relative strength multiway is poor. This is the overvaluing-one-pair trap in its purest form. Bet small for protection at most, and be fully prepared to fold to significant action. Do not stack off a single pair here.
